1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	
41	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
42	
43	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
44	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
45	
46	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
47	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
48	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
49	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
50	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
51	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
52	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
53	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
54	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
55	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
56	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
57	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
58	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
59	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
60	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
61	    blueprint depends on):
62	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
63	       same-iter fast path below).
64	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
65	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
66	       flagged.
67	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
68	       writer was asked to fix.
69	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
70	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
71	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
72	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
73	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
74	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
75	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
76	    still fails, F stays deferred.
77	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
78	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
79	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
80	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
81	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
82	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
83	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
84	
85	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
86	
87	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
88	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
89	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
90	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
91	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
92	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
93	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
94	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
95	    in the blueprint.
96	
97	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
98	
99	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
100	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
101	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
102	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
103	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
104	---
105	
106	# Blueprint Reviewer
107	
108	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
109	
110	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
111	
112	## Your Job
113	
114	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
115	
116	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
117	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
118	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
119	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
120	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
121	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
122	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
123	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
124	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
125	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
126	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
127	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
128	
129	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
130	
131	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
132	
133	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
134	
135	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
136	
137	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematicaly correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
138	
139	## Always read everything
140	
141	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
142	
143	## Directive Format
144	
145	```markdown
146	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
147	
148	## Slug
149	<slug>
150	
151	## Strategy snapshot
152	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy.>
153	
154	## Routes
155	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
156	
157	## References
158	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
159	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
160	
161	## Focus areas (optional)
162	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
163	
164	## Known issues
165	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
166	```
167	
168	## What you do
169	
170	1. **Read your directive completely.**
171	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
172	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
173	   - Read the entire chapter.
174	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
175	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
176	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
177	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
178	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
179	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
180	7. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
181	
182	You may also use:
183	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
184	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
185	
186	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
187	
188	## Report format
189	
190	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
191	
192	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
193	
194	Concretely:
195	
196	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
197	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
198	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
199	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
200	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
201	
202	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
203	
204	```markdown
205	# Blueprint Review Report
206	
207	## Slug
208	<slug>
209	
210	## Iteration
211	<NNN>
212	
213	## Top-level summaries
214	
215	### Incomplete parts
216	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
217	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
218	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
219	
220	### Proofs lacking detail
221	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
222	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
223	
224	### Lean difficulty quality
225	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
226	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
227	
228	### Multi-route coverage
229	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
230	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
231	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
232	
233	### Citation discipline
234	<bullets naming chapter + label + which citation element is missing or suspect. Omit when zero findings.>
235	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: `% SOURCE:` line has no `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical — writer did not name the local file. Likely fabrication.
236	- `Bar.tex` / `\def:scheme`: `% SOURCE:` claims `(read from references/hartshorne-II-1.md)` but that file does not exist on disk. Fabrication; writer must dispatch a retriever or remove the citation.
237	- `Baz.tex` / `\def:etale`: `% SOURCE QUOTE:` is in English but the cited source is [EGA IV, §17.6] (French). Quote was translated — re-extract verbatim in French.
238	- `Qux.tex` / `\thm:gaga`: `% SOURCE QUOTE:` uses the project's notation $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ throughout but the cited source [Serre, GAGA] uses $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. Quote was rewritten — re-extract verbatim with original notation.
239	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before `\begin{proof}` — proof prose reads as a direct translation of Hartshorne's argument; should have the verbatim source proof in a comment.
240	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: visible `\textit{Source: ...}` line claims `Hartshorne III.6.2` but `% SOURCE:` pointer says `III.5.1`. Pointer drift — one of them is wrong.
241	- `Foo.tex`: `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/vakil-ch24.md)` in 3 blocks, but the writer's report's "References consulted" list does not mention `vakil-ch24.md`. The writer cited a file they did not open — fabrication.
242	
243	## Per-chapter
244	
245	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
246	- **complete**: true | partial | false
247	- **correct**: true | partial | false
248	- **notes**:
249	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
250	  - ...
251	
252	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex
253	- **complete**: ...
254	- **correct**: ...
255	- **notes**:
256	  - ...
257	
258	(Cover every chapter. Chapters with findings get the full multi-line
259	block above. Chapters that are `complete: true`, `correct: true`, with
260	no notes worth surfacing get the compact one-liner from the omit-empty
261	rule — do NOT pad them out with `notes: -` or `notes: no drift`.)
262	
263	## Cross-chapter notes
264	
265	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
266	- `<chapter A>` `\lean{Foo.bar}` references a declaration that no longer exists in `Foo.lean` (renamed?).
267	- ...
268	
269	(Use this section for findings that span multiple chapters. Omit if empty.)
270	
271	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
272	
273	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here. These take precedence over everything else; the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md before any Lean work this iter.
274	
275	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>. Resolving requires either changing the strategy or redefining `foo`.
276	
277	## Severity summary
278	
279	Apply these rules verbatim — they decide whether the plan agent dispatches a blueprint-writing subagent (per the catalog) this iter, or defers.
280	
281	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
282	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
283	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
284	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter. A `partial` chapter cannot be relied on by any prover; the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent must be dispatched.
285	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
286	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels — these silently corrupt the dependency graph and must be fixed before provers downstream of them run.
287	  - **Citation-discipline findings on blocks feeding an active prover route**: a missing or suspicious-looking `% SOURCE QUOTE:` on a definition / theorem whose `\lean{...}` is in PROGRESS.md. Formalizing an unverified statement is the iter-149 failure mode; the catalog's literature/reference-fetching subagent must be dispatched before the prover runs.
288	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
289	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
290	  - Citation-discipline findings on blocks NOT feeding an active prover route — still must be resolved eventually, but the project can ship one more iter without them.
291	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues, missing `\texttt{...}` decoration mentions, etc.
292	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
293	
294	Overall verdict: one sentence.
295	```
296	
297	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter (see dispatcher_notes for the gate rule). 1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103

105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.
43	
44	\medskip
45	
46	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
47	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
49	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
50	
51	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
52	
53	\begin{lemma}
54	
55	\leanok
56	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
57	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
58	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
59	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
60	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
61	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
62	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
63	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
64	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
65	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
66	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
67	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
68	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
69	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
70	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
71	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
72	\end{lemma}
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
75	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
76	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
77	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
78	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
79	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
80	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
81	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
82	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
83	% assertion."

90	% rigidity_core -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) and GENUINELY CONSUMED in the body of
91	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open (proves y0 \notin G, the load-bearing non-emptiness of V := Y - G).
92	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).

94	% k-bar-point y0, is closed). rigidity_snd_lift and snd_left_isClosedMap are axiom-clean (no sorryAx).

98	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.

100	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:

102	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
103	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
104	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
105	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual
106	%     (bridge 2's body is real assembly: Step 2 proven, the JacobsonSpace U instance now derivable).
107	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
108	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
109	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
110	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
111	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
112	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
113	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.

115	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
116	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
117	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
118	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
119	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
120	
121	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
122	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
123	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
124	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
125	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
126	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
127	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
128	
129	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
130	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
131	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
132	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
133	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
134	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
135	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
136	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
137	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
138	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
139	\end{proof}

141	\begin{remark}
142	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
143	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
144	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
145	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
146	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
147	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
148	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
149	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
150	  irreducible.)
151	\end{remark}

153	\begin{remark}
154	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}
155	  The Lean formalisation of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} splits into three helper steps, mirroring
156	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
157	  collapsed map $(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$ followed by $f$ with $g \circ p_2$);
158	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
159	  reduced $X \times Y$ into the separated $Z$ that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
160	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
161	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
162	  open $X \times V$ and the slice-constancy of $f$ on it). The collapse hypothesis
163	  $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$ must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
164	  what makes $V$ non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
165	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).
166	
167	  \emph{Status (iter-161).} Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) is \textbf{built} as the axiom-clean
168	  helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap}. The fibre-over-$y_0$ fact is \textbf{closed} (via the
169	  coarse \texttt{PullbackCarrier} layer), so \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open} is
170	  \texttt{sorry}-free in its own body. Bridge~2 (slice-constancy on the saturated open $U$,
171	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}) has now been \textbf{decomposed} along its
172	  cohomology-free route-(c) split into two named sub-lemmas: the globalisation
173	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced
174	  Jacobson source $\Rightarrow$ equal'') is \textbf{proven axiom-clean}, and the per-closed-slice
175	  constancy \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine} (Step~1, Mumford's slice
176	  argument) is the chain's \emph{single genuinely-deep residual} \texttt{sorry}. The body of
177	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} is therefore real assembly (Step~2 over Step~1).
178	
179	  This route requires the base field to be algebraically closed \emph{and} the source to be of finite
180	  type: Step~2 needs the closed points of $U$ to be dense (the Jacobson-space property), which over
181	  the algebraically closed $\bar k$ holds because $X \times Y$ is locally of finite type.
182	  Accordingly the formalization carries \textbf{both} $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and
183	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ across the chain lemmas

194	\begin{lemma}
195	
196	\leanok
197	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
198	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
200	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
202	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
203	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
204	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
205	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
206	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
207	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
208	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
209	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
210	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
211	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
212	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
213	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
214	  Then there is a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq X \times Y$ on which $f$ agrees, as a
215	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
216	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
217	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
218	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
219	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
220	
221	  \medskip
222	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
223	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
224	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
225	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
226	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
227	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
228	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
229	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
230	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
231	
232	  \medskip
233	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
234	  source.} The prose already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
235	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
236	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field $\kappa(y)$ to be $\bar k$ itself, so that a
237	  proper integral slice has global sections $\Gamma = \bar k$ and maps to a single $\bar k$-point of
238	  the affine $U_0$; this requires $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$. Second, the globalisation of the
239	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
240	  closed points of the saturated open $U$ to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ $U$ to be a Jacobson space.
241	  Over the algebraically closed $\bar k$ this holds precisely because $X \times Y$ is \emph{locally of
242	  finite type} over $\bar k$ (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
243	  property of $\Spec \bar k$, and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
244	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$. Accordingly the formalization carries
245	  \emph{both} $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and
246	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ on this lemma (in addition to
247	  $[\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]$), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
248	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
249	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
250	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
251	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
252	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
253	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ --- the base-field variable is literally named $\mathtt{kbar}$ ---
254	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over $\bar k$, so the
255	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
256	\end{lemma}

268	\begin{proof}
269	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
270	  \leanok
271	  Let $U_0$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U_0$, and set
272	  $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a
273	  \emph{closed} map, so $G$ (the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$) is closed in $Y$. The
274	  collapse hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0$ gives
275	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, hence $y_0 \notin G$; this is the only place
276	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for $V := Y - G$ is non-empty precisely
277	  because $y_0 \in V$. For each $y \in V$ the proper variety $X \times \{y\}$ is mapped by $f$
278	  into the affine $U_0$, hence to a single point, necessarily $f(x_0, y)$. So for all $x \in X$,
279	  $y \in V$, we have $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$, i.e.\ $f$ and
280	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on the non-empty open $U := X \times V$. (In the Lean
281	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
282	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
283	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
284	
285	  \medskip
286	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
287	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
288	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
289	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
290	
291	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``$X$ complete $\Rightarrow p_2$ closed'' is
292	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
293	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on $X$, base-changed across the canonical pullback square
294	  to make $p_2$ universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
295	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites $(\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}$ to
296	  $\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}$ exactly. This step is already
297	  proven.
298	
299	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of $V$) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
300	  $X \times \{y_0\}$ over the $\bar k$-rational point $y_0$ is exactly the image of the slice
301	  section $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
302	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
303	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
304	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
305	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1$ and
306	  $s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0$ come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
307	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
308	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
309	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
310	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]$.
311	
312	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
313	  ``Proper connected slice into affine $\Rightarrow$ single point, glued to a morphism equality on
314	  $U$'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
315	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ statement: the
316	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no $H^0$ base change), and route~(c)
317	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
318	  stack is: for each closed point $y \in V$ one has $\kappa(y) = \bar k$ (alg-closed, finite type);
319	  the slice $X_y$ is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
320	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over $\bar k$
321	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
322	  extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring
323	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so $f$ and
324	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
325	  locally-of-finite-type $U$ (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
326	  locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
327	  hypothesis $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$), so the per-slice
328	  agreement globalises to all of $U$ via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
329	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
330	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
331	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
332	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
333	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
334	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
335	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
336	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
337	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
338	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
339	\end{proof}

363	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
364	  ``for each $y \in V$, the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
365	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $X$, $Y$, $Z$ be objects of
366	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ with $X$ proper, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible, reduced,
367	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over $\bar k$ (the formalization carries
368	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$, which makes the saturated open $U$
369	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
370	  consumes), and $Z$ separated. Let $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a $\bar k$-point
371	  $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
372	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$ for the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
373	  endomorphism. Suppose:
374	  \begin{itemize}
375	    \item $U \subseteq X \times Y$ is a $p_2$-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
376	      for a set $V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y$ (so $U$ contains the whole fibre $X_y$ over each of
377	      its points);
378	    \item $U_0 \subseteq Z$ is an \emph{affine} open;
379	    \item $f(U) \subseteq U_0$, i.e.\ $f$ maps $U$ into the affine $U_0$.
380	  \end{itemize}
381	  Then $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on $U$ as scheme morphisms:
382	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$.
383	\end{lemma}
384	
385	\begin{proof}
386	
387	\leanok
388	  \uses{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
389	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each $y \in V$, the complete
390	  variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single
391	  point of $U$''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
392	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
393	  $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
394	  $H^0$ base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
395	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
396	
397	  \medskip
398	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
399	  Fix a closed point $x$ of $U$, lying over a closed point $y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$.
400	  Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type over $\bar k$, the residue field
401	  is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Because $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ is saturated, $U$ contains the
402	  \emph{entire} fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$; hence $f$ maps the proper integral slice $X_y$ into the
403	  affine $U_0$. By $\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$ the global sections $\Gamma(X_y)$ form a
404	  field, module-finite over $\bar k$ by $\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$, and
405	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into
406	  the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so the
407	  restriction of $f$ to $X_y$ factors through a single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$ --- necessarily the
408	  point $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)$. Thus $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$
409	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of $U$. This per-slice statement is the
410	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
411	
412	  \medskip
413	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
414	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-scheme $U$
415	  ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$ / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
416	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$). To turn ``$f$ and
417	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on $U$,
418	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
419	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
420	  $\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U$, whose range is dense --- and feeds
421	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (the same
422	  family $\mathtt{rigidity\_core}$ uses). This yields the morphism equality
423	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$ on all of $U$. The
424	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
425	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
426	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$); that bespoke build is now done in
427	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
428	\end{proof}
429	
430	\begin{lemma}[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
431	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
432	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
433	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
434	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
435	  Let $W$, $Z$ be schemes with $W$ \emph{reduced}, $W$ a \emph{Jacobson space}, and $Z$
436	  \emph{separated}. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z$ be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
437	  $x \in W$, the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
438	  $\Spec \kappa(x) \to W$ (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
439	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$), then $g_1 = g_2$.
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  This is the ``dense closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
444	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
445	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
446	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
447	  dominant probe.
448	
449	  Form the coproduct $P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)$ and the morphism
450	  $\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W$ defined by $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$ of the residue-field points
451	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$. Its topological range contains every closed point of $W$:
452	  the inclusion $\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P$ composes with $\mathrm{probe}$ to
453	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ (the defining property of $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$), and the
454	  range of $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ is the singleton $\{x\}$
455	  ($\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}$). Because $W$ is a Jacobson space, the closed points
456	  are dense ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$, i.e.\ $\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W$), so
457	  the range of $\mathrm{probe}$ is dense and $\mathrm{probe}$ is \emph{dominant}.
458	
459	  Componentwise, $g_1$ and $g_2$ agree after $\mathrm{probe}$: precomposing with each coproduct
460	  inclusion $\iota_x$ reduces (by $\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}$) to
461	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$,
462	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point $x$. By the universal property of the coproduct
463	  ($\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}$) this gives $\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2$.
464	  Since $\mathrm{probe}$ is dominant and $Z$ is separated, $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ cancels it
465	  on the left to yield $g_1 = g_2$.
466	\end{proof}
467	
468	\begin{lemma}[Step~1: per-closed-slice constancy of a proper slice into an affine]
469	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
470	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
471	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
472	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
473	  % $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
474	  % any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
475	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
476	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
477	  ``for each $y \in V$, the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
478	  With the data of \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} --- $\bar k$ algebraically
479	  closed, $X$ proper over $\bar k$, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible, reduced, and locally of
480	  finite type, $Z$ separated, a $\bar k$-point $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, a morphism
481	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$, a $p_2$-saturated open $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ mapping into an
482	  affine open $U_0 \subseteq Z$ --- fix a \emph{closed} point $x$ of $U$. Then $f$ and the collapsed
483	  map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ (with $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
484	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$, i.e.\ $(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$) agree at $x$
485	  after the residue-field probe $\Spec \kappa(x) \to U$:
486	  \[ U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi f)
487	     = U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)). \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	
490	\begin{proof}
491	  This is Mumford's per-slice step, realised \emph{cohomology-free} (route~B): the relative Stein /
492	  proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap and is
493	  deliberately avoided. The closed point $x$ of $U$ lies over a closed point
494	  $y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$; since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type
495	  over $\bar k$, the residue field is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Saturation $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
496	  puts the \emph{entire} proper integral fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$ inside $U$, so $f$ maps $X_y$
497	  into the affine $U_0$. Because $X_y$ is proper integral over $\bar k$, its global sections form a
498	  field ($\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$), module-finite over $\bar k$
499	  ($\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$); algebraic closedness collapses the finite
500	  extension, giving $\Gamma(X_y, \mathcal O) = \bar k$. A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by
501	  its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so $f|_{X_y}$ factors through a
502	  single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$. That point is $f(x_0, y)$, since the $\bar k$-point $(x_0, y)$ lies
503	  on $X_y$; and $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)$ by the definition of $\mathtt{retract}$.
504	  Evaluating at the residue-field probe of the closed point $x$ gives the asserted equality. This
505	  per-slice statement is the chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}; its globalisation
506	  over the dense closed points is \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}.
507	\end{proof}

509	\section{The theorem of the cube (deferred deep input)}
510	
511	\textbf{Deferred prerequisite.} The block below records the theorem of the cube as the heavy,
512	\emph{not-yet-built} characteristic-free input that the single-curve base case of
513	\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} ultimately rests on. We give Mumford's statement
514	verbatim but \emph{no} formal proof: in Mumford the proof spans \S6 (the splitting
515	$T(X_0 \times \cdots \times X_n) = \operatorname{Im}\alpha \oplus \operatorname{Ker}\beta$
516	exhibiting $\Pic$ as a quadratic functor, then the seesaw principle), and the
517	characteristic-free scheme-theoretic form is Chapter~III \S10. This is the one genuinely
518	deep, un-formalised prerequisite of route~(c); it carries no Lean target yet.
519	
520	\begin{theorem}
521	[Theorem of the cube]
522	  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
523	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Theorem (of the cube), Ch.~II \S6 ``The theorem of the cube: I'', book p.~55 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 66)
524	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM. Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties, $Z$ any variety and $x_0$,
525	  % $y_0$ and $z_0$ base points on $X$, $Y$, and $Z$, respectively. If $L$ is any line bundle
526	  % on $X \times Y \times Z$ whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$,
527	  % $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$ and $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, $L$ is trivial."
528	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S6, Theorem of the cube, p.~55.}
529	  Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties and $Z$ any variety over $\bar k$, with base points
530	  $x_0 \in X$, $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$. If $L$ is a line bundle on $X \times Y \times Z$
531	  whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$, $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$, and
532	  $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, then $L$ is trivial. \emph{(Recorded as a deferred
533	  input; no formal proof in this project yet --- this is the heavy un-built prerequisite of
534	  route~(c).)}
535	\end{theorem}

537	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
538	
539	\begin{proposition}
540	
541	\leanok
542	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
543	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
544	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
545	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
546	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
547	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
548	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
549	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
550	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
551	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
552	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
553	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
554	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
555	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
556	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
557	  single $\bar k$-point.
558	\end{proposition}
559	
560	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
561	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
562	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
563	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
564	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
565	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
566	\begin{proof}
567	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
568	  \leanok
569	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
570	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.
571	
572	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
573	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
574	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
575	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
576	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).
577	
578	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
579	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
580	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
581	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
582	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
583	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
584	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
585	  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
586	  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
587	  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
588	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
589	  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
590	  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
591	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)
592	
593	  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
594	  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
595	  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
596	  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
597	  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
598	  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
599	\end{proof}
600	
601	\begin{remark}
602	  \label{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}
603	  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
604	  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
605	  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of
606	  Proposition~3.10 and which proves that maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to
607	  translation. But the \emph{single-curve} base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' is
608	  not a formal consequence of the Rigidity Lemma by itself; in characteristic-free form it
609	  rests on \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}. This refines the iter-156 expectation that a single
610	  $\mathbb P^1$ factor would be cube-free: the multi-factor \emph{induction} is cube-free
611	  (it is pure Rigidity-Lemma additivity), but the base of that induction is not. See the
612	  Strategy-modifying findings of this chapter's writer report.
613	\end{remark}

615	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
616	
617	\begin{proposition}
618	
619	\leanok
620	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
621	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
622	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
623	  \uses{def:genus}
624	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
625	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
626	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
627	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
628	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
629	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
630	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
631	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
632	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
633	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
634	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
635	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
636	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
637	\end{proposition}
638	
639	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
640	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
641	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
642	\begin{proof}
643	  \uses{def:genus}
644	  \leanok
645	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
646	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
647	
648	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
649	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
650	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
651	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
652	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
653	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
654	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
655	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
656	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
657	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
658	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
659	\end{proof}
660	
661	\begin{remark}
662	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
663	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
664	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
665	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
666	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
667	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
668	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
669	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
670	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
671	\end{remark}
